name: Pct-encoded triplets in literal text are emitted verbatim

cases.name: Pct-encoded path separators in surrounding literal are preserved as-is
cases.reason: Literal text including pct-encoded triplets is emitted verbatim — the parser MUST NOT re-encode `%2F` to `%252F` nor decode it to `/`.

cases.name: Lowercase hex in a literal pct-encoded triplet is preserved without normalization
cases.reason: RFC 6570 says hex digits are case-insensitive, but literals are emitted verbatim. The lowercase `%2f` MUST stay lowercase in the output even though the trailing `%2F` is uppercase.

cases.name: Pct-encoded percent sign in a literal does not become double-encoded
cases.reason: `%25` is the literal pct-encoded form of `%`. Because it appears in literal text (not in an expanded value), it is NOT re-encoded into `%2525`.

cases.name: Multi-octet UTF-8 sequence written as pct-encoded literals stays untouched
cases.reason: The literal `%ED%8E%98%EB%94%94%ED%8C%8C%EC%9D%B4` (the UTF-8 encoding of `페디파이`) consists of twelve pct-encoded triplets. The parser must accept consecutive pct-encoded literals and emit them unchanged.

cases.name: Pct-encoded operator-like characters in a literal do not introduce another expression
cases.reason: `%23` is the encoded `#`, which is forbidden as a bare literal but ALLOWED as a pct-encoded triplet. It must NOT trigger fragment-style expansion behavior.

cases.name: Pct-encoded reserved characters appear in literal even though their decoded forms are forbidden
cases.reason: The bare characters `<`, `>`, `\`, `^`, `` ` ``, `|` are forbidden in literals, but their pct-encoded forms `%3C`, `%3E`, `%5C`, `%5E`, `%60`, `%7C` are allowed verbatim.

name: Pct-encoded brackets in literals are not delimiters

cases.name: Encoded `{` and `}` surrounding a varname are literal text, no expansion happens
cases.reason: `%7B`/`%7D` are pct-encoded literals — they look like brackets to a human but are NOT expression delimiters. The whole template is one literal; `var` is never looked up.

cases.name: Encoded brackets surrounding a real expression form decorative literals
cases.reason: Only the unencoded `{`/`}` denote the expression. `%7B` before and `%7D` after are literal text — useful when you actually want braces around an expanded value.

cases.name: Encoded close-brace inside literal does not terminate a previous open expression
cases.reason: After the expression `{var}` closes, the literal `%7Dtail` follows. The encoded `%7D` is ordinary literal text and does not interact with bracket parsing.

cases.name: Encoded brackets adjacent to real brackets must not be confused
cases.reason: Doubled encoded brackets are still literal. The single real expression `{var}` expands; the two encoded `%7B%7B` and `%7D%7D` flank it as decoration.

cases.name: Encoded brackets between two expressions do not nest or escape anything
cases.reason: The two expressions are independent and concatenate; the encoded `%7B%7D` literal sits between them like any other literal text.

cases.name: Varname containing a pct-encoded space is looked up verbatim
cases.reason: Per RFC 6570 ABNF, `varchar = ALPHA / DIGIT / "_" / pct-encoded`. So `abc%20def` is one valid varname. The lookup uses the pct-encoded form as the key — it is NOT decoded to `abc def`.

cases.name: Varname starting with a pct-encoded character is not interpreted as an operator
cases.reason: The first character of the expression is `%`, which is not an operator. The varname is the entire pct-encoded string `%2Bvar` — it must NOT be confused with the `+` operator (whose pct-encoded form is `%2B`).

cases.name: Pct-encoded varname is preserved when emitted by a named operator
cases.reason: Named operators echo the varname using "the same encoding process as for literals". A pct-encoded triplet inside the varname is therefore preserved verbatim — neither decoded nor double-encoded.

cases.name: Varname with an internal dot is one varname, not two joined by `.`
cases.reason: ABNF: `varname = varchar *( ["."] varchar )`. Dots are part of the varname, so `foo.bar` is the literal lookup key — NOT a path into nested objects.

cases.name: Varname consisting only of digits is valid
cases.reason: `varchar` includes DIGIT, so a varname may start with — and consist entirely of — digits.

cases.name: Pct-encoded varname combined with a normal varname in one expression
cases.reason: Two independent varspecs joined by `,`. The first uses a pct-encoded varname; the lookup is independent of how varnames are written.

cases.name: Varname that looks like an encoded close-brace is a valid (undefined) varname
cases.reason: RFC 6570 §2.3: "A varname MAY contain one or more pct-encoded triplets." So `%7D` is a single pct-encoded varchar — and a complete, valid varname. With no `%7D` key defined, the varname is undefined per §3.2.1 and the expression collapses to an empty string.

cases.name: Varname that looks like an encoded open-brace resolves to its defined value
cases.reason: Symmetric to the undefined `%7D` case: `%7B` is a single pct-encoded varchar and a complete varname. With `%7B` mapped to `open-brace` in the variable set, the expression expands to that defined value — the encoded-bracket appearance does NOT prevent normal lookup.

cases.name: Varname spelled in pct-encoded form is NOT the same as its decoded counterpart
cases.reason: RFC 6570 §2.3: "A varname containing pct-encoded characters is not the same variable as a varname with those same characters decoded." So `%41` and `A` are two distinct variable names. The lookup uses `%41` literally and yields `encoded-A`, not whatever `A` might (or might not) resolve to.

cases.name: Pct-encoded varname under a named operator emits the encoded form as the parameter name
cases.reason: Named-operator output echoes the varname "using the same encoding process as for literals". Since `%41` is already a valid pct-encoded literal triplet, it is preserved verbatim — the parameter name in the URI is literally `%41`, not `A`.

cases.name: Null entries in a list are skipped during expansion
cases.reason: RFC 6570 §2.3 says "a list... a null... is considered undefined". The null at index 1 is skipped; only `one` and `three` are joined by `,`.

cases.name: Null entries in a list are skipped under named explode too
cases.reason: Null members are excluded from the iteration entirely. Only the two defined members emit `sparse=…` pairs joined by `&`.

cases.name: Null entries do not produce an empty path segment
cases.reason: Each defined member produces one path segment. The null member is skipped, so the output has TWO segments — NOT three with an empty middle one.

cases.name: Null values in an associative array drop their key
cases.reason: The pair `(b, null)` is excluded because its value is undefined. Only `a=1` and `c=3` survive, flattened as `name,value` joined by `,`.

cases.name: Null values are skipped under named explode of an associative array
cases.reason: Pairs with undefined values are skipped before the `?`/`&` separator logic runs. The output looks identical to an object that simply did not contain key `b`.

name: Explode modifier on string values is silently ignored

cases.name: Explode on a string under NUL is a no-op
cases.reason: The expansion algorithm dispatches on value type. For string values, the explode flag is irrelevant — the result is identical to `{var}`.

cases.name: Explode on a string under named operator does not multiply assignments
cases.reason: Strings have no "members" to explode over. The output is the same as `{?var}`. People often expect `?var=v&var=a&var=l&var=u&var=e` — that is wrong.

cases.name: Explode on an empty string is also a no-op
cases.reason: Empty string is a defined string value. The string-branch logic runs and emits `name=` because of `ifemp = =`. The `*` has no effect.

cases.name: Explode on a path-like string still encodes reserved characters under NUL
cases.reason: Same string-branch — `*` is ignored. Encoding rules of NUL still apply: each `/` becomes `%2F`.

cases.name: Explode on a string with prefix-length-exceeding value is still a single value
cases.reason: `*` is silently ignored on the string `hello`. The result is the same as `{hello}`.

name: Mismatched pct-encoded brackets and stray brackets

cases.name: Bare closing brace in literals is forbidden even when an encoded open brace precedes it
cases.reason: `%7B` is a pct-encoded literal — it does NOT open an expression. The trailing bare `}` is forbidden in literals (allowed character set excludes `}`), so the template is malformed.

cases.name: Real opening brace closed only by an encoded brace is unclosed
cases.reason: Inside the expression, `%7D` is a valid pct-encoded varchar — it becomes part of the varname `var%7D`. There is no actual `}` to terminate the expression, so parsing fails.

cases.name: Stray bare close-brace before any literal text or expression fails
cases.reason: The leading `}` is a bare close-brace in the literals position. RFC 6570 §2.1 forbids `}` from the literals character set regardless of what follows — the trailing encoded `%7B%7D` does not rescue the template.

cases.name: Encoded open brace followed by stray real close brace fails on the close brace
cases.reason: The literal `%7B` is fine. The following bare `}` is forbidden in literals.

cases.name: Real opening brace nested inside a varname-shaped sequence is invalid
cases.reason: The expression body becomes `var%7B%7D` — three pct-encoded characters all valid as varchars — but the expression never sees a literal `}`. Unclosed.

cases.name: Prefix on a list is not applicable per spec
cases.reason: RFC 6570 §2.4.1 states: "Prefix modifiers are not applicable to variables that have composite values." `list` is composite, so this must fail at expansion time.

cases.name: Prefix on an associative array is not applicable per spec
cases.reason: Same rule as for lists: prefix length is undefined for object values.

cases.name: Prefix on a list under named operator also fails
cases.reason: The named operator does not bypass the §2.4.1 restriction. The prefix modifier remains inapplicable regardless of the surrounding operator.

cases.name: Prefix on a path-segment expansion of a composite still fails
cases.reason: Path-segment operator does not change the type of the variable. Composite means composite.

cases.name: Prefix on the canonical `count` list also fails
cases.reason: `count` is `["one", "two", "three"]`. Prefix modifier is invalid for it. The error is at expansion time, not parsing time — the syntax `count:2` is grammatically well-formed.
